current_fact.fact_text: On April 4, 1975, a U.S. Air Force C-5A Galaxy carrying hundreds of Vietnamese orphans as part of Operation Babylift crashed shortly after takeoff near Saigon. Although many were killed, rescuers pulled survivors from the wreckage, turning a mission meant to symbolize rescue into a complex story of loss, controversy, and courage.

episode.title: Morning Drops #43 - Looking Back
episode.description:
In this first Morning Drop of 2026, Yasmin opens a space for quiet reflection on the nature of transitions. The episode traces a personal journey that began three years ago, when a sudden injury on New Year’s Day turned a desire for movement into a mandatory season of stillness.

Through the story of a broken knee and the slow process of relearning to walk, the narrative explores the difference between being strong and being aware. It touches on the vulnerability of the body, the unexpected joy found in daily progress, and the warmth that comes from finally allowing oneself to accept help. The musical selection mirrors this path of recovery, moving from the uncertainty of change to the steady rhythm of healing.

Mood: Reflective, Resilient, Grounded
Music genre: Indie Pop, Cinematic, World, Soul

We invite you to listen deeply and find comfort in your own pace.
songs: Kelly Finnigan - I Don't Wanna Wait
Goran Bregović & Iggy Pop - This Is a Film

current_fact.match_reason: A mission marked by trauma, survival, and lifelong scars resonates with an episode about healing after sudden injury, both honoring slow recovery and complex memory.

queue.fact_text: On April 4, 1973, a Lockheed C-141 Starlifter nicknamed the “Hanoi Taxi” completed the last flight of Operation Homecoming, bringing American prisoners of war back from North Vietnam. For those on board, the aircraft’s cargo bay was less a cabin and more a corridor between captivity and an uncertain homecoming.

episode.title: Morning Drops #14 - Slow Planet
episode.description: A pause. A quiet return to slower rhythms, sparked by a live set under the summer sky. A moment to breathe and settle into the week.

queue.match_reason: A final flight carrying prisoners home into a new, slower life pairs with a mellow, downtempo mix about easing into gentler rhythms after intense chapters.

queue.fact_text: On April 4, 1944, Allied forces launched their first major bombardment of oil refineries around Bucharest, Romania, killing thousands of civilians. The raids targeted the fuel that powered war machines, showing how modern conflict often hits the invisible systems that keep everyday life running.

episode.title: Morning Drops #49 - Rage Against The Machine
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin revisits the story of Rage Against the Machine — a band born in tension, shaped by conviction, and remembered for moments that blurred the line between stage and protest.
From early Los Angeles unrest to Wall Street performances, this episode moves through rupture, resistance, and legacy — carried by songs that still feel urgent today.
Listen closely.
songs: Rage Against the Machine - Killing in the Name
Rage Against the Machine - Without a Face
Rage Against the Machine - Guerrilla Radio
Rage Against the Machine - Sleep Now in the Fire

queue.match_reason: Bombing oil refineries in a total war economy fits an episode steeped in political critique and explosive sound, both questioning the systems that fuel conflict.

queue.fact_text: On April 4, 1814, Napoleon Bonaparte first conditionally abdicated the French throne in favor of his young son Napoleon II, before surrendering unconditionally two days later. For a man who had redrawn Europe, it was a moment of forced reinvention and the beginning of an exile that turned a ruler into a legend.

episode.title: Morning Drops #30 - Star Treatment
episode.description: A celebration of change and transformation. Gaia traces the evolution of Arctic Monkeys - from raw energy to cinematic sound - reflecting on how artists, and all of us, grow and change through time. A journey through courage, curiosity, and the beauty of slowing down.
songs: Arctic Monkeys - There’d Better Be a Mirrorball

queue.match_reason: Napoleon’s fall from emperor to exile is a dramatic arc of transformation, matching an episode about evolution, changing identity, and adjusting to a slower, more reflective phase.

queue.fact_text: On April 4, 1818, the U.S. Congress adopted a new design for the national flag: 13 stripes and a star for each state, which at that time meant 20 stars in a growing constellation. The pattern quietly encoded expansion, leaving room in the sky for more stars yet to join.

episode.title: Morning Drops #17 - Steel City
episode.description: Gaia takes us on a short trip through Sheffield's sounds — from electronic textures to raw guitars and sharp storytelling. Four tracks, one city, different edges of the same story.
songs: The Human League - The Sound Of The Crowd

queue.match_reason: A flag redesign that maps identity onto stars and stripes pairs with an episode about a city’s sonic identity—each shows how a place tells its story through symbols and sound.

queue.fact_text: On April 4, 1958, the now-iconic peace symbol of Britain’s Campaign for Nuclear Disarmament was displayed publicly for the first time in London. Designed from semaphore signals for “N” and “D”, it fused code and art into one of the most recognizable protest icons on Earth.

episode.title: Morning Drops #04 - Bars With Balls
episode.description: Yasmin dives into Women's Day through the voices of four incredible female rappers. Morning fire from the magic planet of Sedna FM — move your hands up and down with us. Hip hop culture through a feminine lens.
songs: Little Simz - Point and Kill (feat. Obongjayar)

queue.match_reason: A symbol born in the streets of protest belongs next to an episode of outspoken women in hip‑hop; both turn art and language into tools for peace and resistance.

queue.fact_text: On April 4, 1963, the film adaptation of the musical "Bye Bye Birdie" was released, satirizing the hysteria around rock ’n’ roll idols drafted into the army. With bright choreography and pop hooks, it captured how teen culture, television, and music were beginning to remix each other in the early 1960s.

episode.title: Morning Drops #12 - 80's Club
episode.description: Starting with a Monday evening in Munich — a few drinks, friends, and landing in an 80s dance party. From Donna Summer's hypnotic pulse, to Depeche Mode's bright synthpop, Ken Laszlo's Italo Disco romance, and Mylène Farmer's darker theatrical world. Four songs, four corners of the 80s — connected by the machines that changed how we dance.
songs: Depeche Mode - Just Can't Get Enough

queue.match_reason: A colorful musical about pop idolatry and youth culture fits an 80s dance-floor homage; both revel in catchy hooks, theatrical flair, and the evolving relationship between music and mass media.

queue.fact_text: On April 4, 1964, The Beatles achieved a chart feat that has never been matched: occupying the top five positions on the U.S. Billboard Hot 100 simultaneously. For one surreal week, American pop radio sounded like a single band’s private universe.

episode.title: Morning Drops #07 - secret_mp3_player_1
episode.description: Inspired by a throwback MP3 player on Instagram, Yasmin and Gaia take you on a nostalgic journey to the early 2000s. LimeWire, eMule, guilty-pleasure playlists — remember those? Hit play and relive the songs that shaped a generation.

queue.match_reason: A week when one band owned the charts mirrors an era when one MP3 player could define your world; both are about the total takeover of youth listening by a handful of irresistible songs.

queue.fact_text: On April 4, 1581, Queen Elizabeth I knighted Francis Drake aboard the Golden Hind after his circumnavigation of the globe. His voyage stitched together oceans and empires, reshaping maps and proving that a wooden ship could orbit an entire planet of water and storms.

episode.title: Evening Flows #01 - Yasmin - Crescendo in the Medina
episode.description: The first Evening Flow episode comes from Yasmin. It starts deep in the medina, slow, mysterious, textured, and grows into a liberating dance across sub-Saharan landscapes. A journey through ambient soundscapes, Arabic melodies, Afro-Latin rhythms, and funky highlife grooves.
songs: David Visan & Carlos Campos - Crystal Heart
Evan Mars - Ya Zareef Al Tool
Dele Sosimi & Medlar - You No Fit Touch Am (Medlar Remix)

queue.match_reason: Drake’s circumnavigation was a global route stitched from ports and cultures, much like this evening mix sails from medina alleys to sub‑Saharan grooves in one continuous voyage.

queue.fact_text: On April 4, 1996, while the world admired Comet Hyakutake from Earth’s night skies, NEAR Shoemaker’s camera caught it from space—a robot eye watching an icy visitor. Its images revealed jets of material erupting from the nucleus, showing a supposedly simple “dirty snowball” behaving like a restless, active world.

episode.title: Evening Flows #00 - Introduction to evening flows
episode.description:
Sedna FM introduces a new format: Evening Flow, where you can enjoy curated music sets from selectors, DJs, and artists.

Each mix is an invitation to drift beyond the expected. A space where hidden gems from personal archives come to light, where sound speaks its own language. There are no rules here, no patterns to follow. Just the steady unfolding of a mood, a texture, a journey shaped by feeling.

Let the music lead the way - enjoy the trip!

queue.match_reason: A probe drifting close to a comet embodies the spirit of Evening Flows: floating beyond the expected, discovering hidden activity in what first seems like a quiet, distant object.

queue.fact_text: On April 4, 2023, Finland officially became a member of NATO, doubling the alliance’s land border with Russia overnight. A country long known for balancing East and West effectively changed its security story with a single signature.

episode.title: Morning Drops #27 - Nomophobia
episode.description: Yasmin reflects on what happened when she recently lost her phone and why it felt freeing. A dive into the psychology of our relationship with smartphones: from nomophobia (the fear of being without a phone), to digital burnout and cognitive overload, to the surprising benefits of boredom and the brain's default mode network. An invitation to rethink how we connect and disconnect.

queue.match_reason: Finland’s pivot into a new alliance reflects a deep rethink of connection and security, echoing an episode that questions how and why we plug into larger networks in our own lives.

queue.fact_text: On April 4, 1904, the paired Kresna earthquakes reminded Europe that its continental crust is still restless, not a solid, sleeping platform. The quakes helped scientists better understand the complex fault networks under the Balkans, turning destruction into data about our planet’s hidden mechanics.

episode.title: Morning Drops #19 - Wabi Sabi
episode.description: Wabi-sabi is a story about a handmade mug, a small mistake, and the idea that imperfection can be something to keep—not correct. In this Morning Drop, Gaia shares a moment that didn’t go to plan, and four tracks that echo the same spirit: rough takes, studio accidents, lo-fi textures, and quiet surprises. A soft, thoughtful selection moving between warm electronics, classic riffs, and raw edges.A reminder that not everything needs to be polished. Sometimes, it’s better that way.

queue.match_reason: Earthquakes expose the cracks beneath our illusion of solidity; an episode about wabi‑sabi finds beauty in cracks and imperfections, turning rupture into a different kind of appreciation.

queue.fact_text: On April 4, 2010, as Baja California shook, sensitive GPS instruments recorded tiny, permanent shifts in Earth’s crust—millimeters here, centimeters there. These subtle displacements showed how a single quake can quietly redraw maps, even far from the epicenter.

episode.title: Morning Drops #39 - Moving Meditation
episode.description: An episode inspired by how travel helps us see more clearly. Stepping into new places opens the mind, shifts perspective. The music moves through balearic warmth, boogie grooves, world and latin-folk influences, and touches of new wave — a selection built to follow the quiet changes that happen when you're out of your usual rhythm.
songs: España Circo Este - Dormo poco e sogno molto
Talking Heads - This Must Be the Place (Naive Melody)

queue.match_reason: Microscopic shifts in the crust after a quake parallel the subtle perspective shifts that travel and movement bring, echoed by this gently percussive, world‑wandering playlist.

queue.fact_text: On April 4, 1969, while Denton Cooley’s temporary artificial heart was making history in Houston, heart surgery itself was still a young science—barely a couple of decades old. The operation pushed the boundary between human tissue and machine, foreshadowing a future where organs might be engineered as much as healed.

episode.title: Sedna FM #06 - Alters of an Ego
episode.description: What if you could step outside yourself and become someone freer, bolder, or completely unexpected? Exploring the artistry of musicians who craft alter-egos and parallel realities to experiment with new dimensions of their identity. It's not about hiding who they are — it's about expanding who they can be.
songs: The Fireman - Strawberries Oceans Ships Forest

queue.match_reason: A mechanical heart temporarily standing in for a human one is a kind of bodily alter‑ego; the episode’s focus on crafted identities and parallel selves mirrors this hybrid, in‑between state of being.

published.fact_text: On April 4, 1968, NASA launched Apollo 6, the second and last uncrewed test flight of the mighty Saturn V rocket. The mission was riddled with dramatic engine oscillations and failures, yet the data it returned helped engineers refine the system that would soon carry humans to the Moon.

episode.title: Morning Drops #03 - Space Shift
episode.description: Gaia invites you to shift your mindset with a simple yet powerful reflection: What if we saw emptiness not as a void but as space to create? An energising and feel-good selection of tracks, the perfect way to ease into your day and set the tone for the week ahead.
songs: Willy J Healey - Woke Up Smiling
The Strokes - You Only Live Once

published.match_reason: A spaceflight stress-test pairs perfectly with an episode about reimagining "empty" space as possibility, mirrored by the propulsive, forward-moving tracks.

published.fact_text: On April 4, 1983, Space Shuttle Challenger lifted off on mission STS-6, its maiden voyage into orbit. Among other tasks, it carried and deployed the first Tracking and Data Relay Satellite, helping weave an invisible communications web around Earth.

episode.title: Sedna FM #02 - Destination Sedna
episode.description: Continuing the Sedna FM journey together with family and friends! Episode two of the main series — exploring new musical territories and deepening the connection with our listeners. A collective voyage through sound.
songs: Odion Iruoje - Identify With Your Roots
Depeche mode - world in my eyes (Oil Tank Mix)

published.match_reason: A shuttle’s first journey and a communications satellite echo the theme of setting off to new destinations and connecting distant listeners, much like this cosmic, exploratory mix.

published.fact_text: On April 4, 1997, Space Shuttle Columbia launched on STS-83, a mission dedicated to microgravity science. A faulty fuel cell forced an early return after just a few days, turning a carefully planned research voyage into a lesson in resilience and rapid problem-solving.

episode.title: Morning Drops #42 - The Joy Of Learning
episode.description:
Hosted by Yasmin, this Morning Drop explores learning as a lifelong process, focusing on persistence, repetition, and the emotional landscape that comes with challenging oneself over time.

Through personal stories and scientific insights, the episode looks at how learning reshapes the brain, generates joy through effort, and why progress often arrives after confusion and frustration. It reflects on the so-called delayed pleasure effect, the role of dopamine in motivation, and how curiosity and learning support long-term wellbeing.

The episode also questions our cultural relationship with failure and invites a softer, more realistic approach to learning, one that accepts mistakes as part of growth and sees persistence as a quiet form of strength.

Mood: reflective, grounded, quietly hopeful
Music genre: alternative rock, hip hop, electronic, neo-soul, drill

A space to listen slowly and stay with the process.

published.match_reason: A mission cut short yet rich in lessons mirrors an episode about finding growth in setbacks and the slow joy of learning from what doesn’t go to plan.

published.fact_text: On April 4, 1996, Comet Hyakutake—one of the great comets of the 1990s—was imaged by NASA’s NEAR Shoemaker probe as it streaked close to Earth. The flyby yielded rare, up-close data on the comet’s dust and gas, turning a fleeting sky spectacle into a scientific windfall.

episode.title: Morning Drops #40 - Diving With Orcas
episode.description: Yasmin's friend Caroline takes us beyond the polar circle, into the Arctic waters of Norway, where orcas and humpback whales gather each winter. A personal journey shaped by a quiet obsession with orcas, from Patagonia to the icy fjords of the north. The soundscape moves gently between cinematic swells and soft, rhythmic pulses — echoing the cold light of Arctic mornings and the grace of underwater encounters.

published.match_reason: A close encounter with a comet parallels intimate encounters with orcas—both are graceful, rare visitors in vast, cold spaces, matched by the episode’s cinematic, luminous mood.

published.fact_text: On April 4, 1969, surgeon Denton Cooley implanted the first temporary artificial heart in a human patient. The device kept the patient alive for three days until a donor heart became available, proving that machines could, at least briefly, stand in for our most vital organ.

episode.title: Morning Drops #47 - The Rise Of Autotune
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin explores the explosive rise of Auto-Tune, a tool that quietly changed the sound of music without asking for attention. From fragile, imperfect recordings to voices shaped with precision, this episode follows the moment when correction became standard and sound became controlled.

Along the way, we discover the curious story of how it was invented, how it moved from the background of studios to the center of debate, and how a few artists turned it into something expressive rather than hidden.

Listen slowly, and let us know what you think about Autotune.
songs: Chet Baker – I Fall in Love Too Easily

published.match_reason: An artificial heart and Auto-Tune both raise deep questions about where the organic ends and technology begins, each quietly reshaping what we consider "natural" life or sound.

published.fact_text: On April 4, 1796, Georges Cuvier delivered what is considered the first true paleontological lecture, arguing that fossils were the remains of extinct species. At a time when many believed nature made no mistakes and nothing truly vanished, he introduced the radical idea of extinction as a force shaping Earth’s history.

episode.title: Morning Drops #31 - A Trip-Hop Journey
episode.description: Hosted by friend Lorenzo, a journey through the roots and evolution of trip-hop. From Bristol's early experiments with Massive Attack and Tricky, to DJ Shadow's cinematic sampling in California, and Thievery Corporation's global downtempo grooves in Washington D.C. — exploring how this genre blurred boundaries and reshaped electronic music.
songs: Tricky - Hell Is Round the Corner
DJ Shadow - Midnight in a Perfect World

published.match_reason: Just as Cuvier pieced together ancient worlds from fragments, trip‑hop builds new soundscapes from sampled shards of the past—both slow, atmospheric acts of reconstruction.

published.fact_text: On April 4, 1904, a pair of powerful earthquakes struck Bulgaria’s Kresna region, reaching magnitudes of about 7.1. These twin shocks are among the strongest recorded in Europe, reshaping local geology and leaving a long seismic memory in the Balkans.

episode.title: Morning Drops #05 - Electric Shock
episode.description: An energizing episode with electrifying sounds to wake you up and get you moving. High energy tracks that deliver a jolt of musical electricity to start your day.

published.match_reason: The sudden, immense energy of twin quakes resonates with an "Electric Shock" set—both unleash raw, shaking power that changes the landscape, whether geological or musical.

published.fact_text: On April 4, 1905, a devastating earthquake struck India’s Kangra Valley, killing around 20,000 people and leveling much of Kangra, McLeod Ganj, and Dharamshala. In the aftermath, the region was literally rebuilt on fractured ground, inspiring new approaches to architecture and risk in the Himalayas.

episode.title: Morning Drops #45 - It Can't Rain Forever
episode.description:
In this first broadcast of 2026, Gaia returns from the vivid landscapes of Morocco to open a space for reflection. This episode navigates the delicate transition between closing a significant chapter and stepping into the new year. It explores the concept of constancy amidst change, celebrating the grounding force of friendship and the creative sanctuary that is Sedna FM.

The musical selection mirrors this journey, moving from the orchestral depth of changing seasons into the golden warmth of psychedelic soul. It recalls memories of Swiss mountains and unexpected downpours, reminding us that shared optimism can weather any storm.

Take a moment to breathe in the new morning and listen deeply.

Mood: Reflective, Warm, Transitional, Hopeful
Music genre: Classic Rock, Psychedelic Soul, Pop Rock, Soft Rock

published.match_reason: A catastrophic quake followed by rebuilding aligns with an episode about storms passing and hope returning, both emphasizing that even deep fractures don’t last forever.

published.fact_text: On April 4, 2010, a magnitude 7.2 earthquake struck just south of the Mexico–United States border, rattling cities on both sides. The quake cracked roads, damaged canals, and subtly shifted the ground beneath millions of people who suddenly realized how alive the Earth really is.

episode.title: Morning Drops #33 - Immerse
episode.description: An analogy between diving, one of Gaia's great passions, and the act of immersing in life itself. A reflection on what happens when we go below the surface: when we let ourselves be fully present. From moments of calm suspension to warm, rhythmic flow, the selection moves like water — fluid, organic, and alive. An invitation to slow down, listen deeper, and float.
songs: Pino D'Angiò - Un Po' d'Uva e un Liquore

published.match_reason: A border‑straddling quake and an episode about immersing in shifting environments both invite us to feel the fluid, changing nature of the ground—whether earth, water, or emotion.

published.fact_text: On April 4, 1933, the giant U.S. Navy airship USS Akron was wrecked off the coast of New Jersey during a storm, killing 73 of the 76 people on board. The disaster effectively ended the U.S. military’s airship ambitions, illustrating how vulnerable even the largest sky-vessels are to weather’s invisible currents.

episode.title: Morning Drops #31 - Sailing With Germans
episode.description: From the deck of a sailing boat, hosted by Yasmin and her special guests. A week at sea with six Germans, each with their own stories, songs, and ways of moving through the waves. From shared meals to unexpected laughter, from quiet resilience to sun-soaked joy — a collection of voices and tracks that reflect what it means to journey together.
songs: Ben Howard - Keep Your Head Up

published.match_reason: A doomed airship ride and an episode set on a sailing boat both explore life carried by air and sea; where Akron shows the risks of the elements, the mix celebrates the fragile joy of traveling within them.

published.fact_text: On April 4, 1975, a U.S. Air Force C-5A Galaxy carrying hundreds of Vietnamese orphans as part of Operation Babylift crashed shortly after takeoff near Saigon. Although many were killed, rescuers pulled survivors from the wreckage, turning a mission meant to symbolize rescue into a complex story of loss, controversy, and courage.
